blocks.text: Caring for the Sick Child in the Community
Participant Manual Compressed Test Edition (~2 000 words)
Based on: WHO/UNICEF 2012 ISBN 978 92 4 154804 5

This compressed edition preserves all clinical decision logic, danger signs, treatment protocols, dosage tables, and follow-up rules from the original 141-page manual. Exercises, role plays, photos, and facilitation notes are omitted. For training use only with the full manual.
Page 1/6

1.raw_text: Caring for the Sick Child in the Community Participant Manual Compressed Test Edition (~2 000 words) Based on: WHO/UNICEF 2012 ISBN 978 92 4 154804 5 This compressed edition preserves all clinical decision logic, danger signs, treatment protocols, dosage tables, and follow-up rules from the original 141-page manual. Exercises, role plays, photos, and facilitation notes are omitted. For training use only with the full manual. Page 1/6

blocks.text: Caring for the Sick Child in the Community (Compressed Test Edition)
1. INTRODUCTION

This manual trains community health workers (CHWs) to assess, classify, and treat sick children age 2 months up to 5 years in the community, as part of the Integrated Management of Childhood Illness (IMCI) strategy. CHWs identify signs of illness, treat diarrhoea, confirmed malaria, and pneumonia (cough with fast breathing) at home, and urgently refer children with danger signs to a health facility.
2. SICK CHILD RECORDING FORM

For every visit, the CHW completes the Sick Child Recording Form. Record: date, CHW name, child's name, age (years and months), sex, caregiver's name and relationship (mother/father/other), and address. The form guides the visit: identify problems, decide to refer or treat, give medicine, advise on home care, and schedule follow-up.
3. IDENTIFY PROBLEMS
ASK the caregiver about the child's problems, then LOOK for signs of illness. The findings determine whether to refer or treat at home.
3.1 ASK: What are the child's problems?
Cough
Ask: Does the child have a cough? If yes, for how long? A cough for 14 days or more is a danger sign.
Diarrhoea
Diarrhoea is 3 or more loose or watery stools in 24 hours. Ask: For how long? Is there blood in the stool? Diarrhoea for 14 days or more is a danger sign. Blood in stool (dysentery) is a danger sign.
Fever

Ask: Does the child have fever now or in the last 3 days? If yes, when did it start? Fever for 7 days or more is a danger sign. Identify fever by the caregiver's report or by feeling the child's stomach or underarm.
Convulsions
A convulsion during the current illness is a danger sign. The child needs urgent referral.
Not able to drink or feed
A child who cannot drink or feed anything at all has a danger sign. Ask the caregiver to offer the child a drink or breastfeed.
Vomiting everything
A child who vomits everything and cannot hold anything down has a danger sign. A child who vomits sometimes but can hold down some fluids does not have this sign.
3.2 LOOK: Signs of illness on examination
Chest indrawing

Look at the lower chest wall when the child breathes IN. Chest indrawing is when the lower chest wall goes IN when the child breathes in. This is a danger sign indicating severe pneumonia. The child must be calm; do not assess while crying or breastfeeding.
Fast breathing
Count the child's breaths for one full minute while the child is calm and quiet. Fast breathing thresholds by age:

blocks.html:
Age groupFast breathing if
2 months up to 12 months50 breaths per minute or more
12 months up to 5 years40 breaths per minute or more
blocks.text: Age group Fast breathing if 2 months up to 12 months 50 breaths per minute or more 12 months up to 5 years 40 breaths per minute or more
Unusually sleepy or unconscious
Page 2/6

2.raw_text: Caring for the Sick Child in the Community (Compressed Test Edition) 1. INTRODUCTION This manual trains community health workers (CHWs) to assess, classify, and treat sick children age 2 months up to 5 years in the community, as part of the Integrated Management of Childhood Illness (IMCI) strategy. CHWs identify signs of illness, treat diarrhoea, confirmed malaria, and pneumonia (cough with fast breathing) at home, and urgently refer children with danger signs to a health facility. 2. SICK CHILD RECORDING FORM For every visit, the CHW completes the Sick Child Recording Form. Record: date, CHW name, child's name, age (years and months), sex, caregiver's name and relationship (mother/father/other), and address. The form guides the visit: identify problems, decide to refer or treat, give medicine, advise on home care, and schedule follow-up. 3. IDENTIFY PROBLEMS ASK the caregiver about the child's problems, then LOOK for signs of illness. The findings determine whether to refer or treat at home. 3.1 ASK: What are the child's problems? Cough Ask: Does the child have a cough? If yes, for how long? A cough for 14 days or more is a danger sign. Diarrhoea Diarrhoea is 3 or more loose or watery stools in 24 hours. Ask: For how long? Is there blood in the stool? Diarrhoea for 14 days or more is a danger sign. Blood in stool (dysentery) is a danger sign. Fever Ask: Does the child have fever now or in the last 3 days? If yes, when did it start? Fever for 7 days or more is a danger sign. Identify fever by the caregiver's report or by feeling the child's stomach or underarm. Convulsions A convulsion during the current illness is a danger sign. The child needs urgent referral. Not able to drink or feed A child who cannot drink or feed anything at all has a danger sign. Ask the caregiver to offer the child a drink or breastfeed. Vomiting everything A child who vomits everything and cannot hold anything down has a danger sign. A child who vomits sometimes but can hold down some fluids does not have this sign. 3.2 LOOK: Signs of illness on examination Chest indrawing Look at the lower chest wall when the child breathes IN. Chest indrawing is when the lower chest wall goes IN when the child breathes in. This is a danger sign indicating severe pneumonia. The child must be calm; do not assess while crying or breastfeeding. Fast breathing Count the child's breaths for one full minute while the child is calm and quiet. Fast breathing thresholds by age: Age group Fast breathing if 2 months up to 12 months 50 breaths per minute or more 12 months up to 5 years 40 breaths per minute or more Unusually sleepy or unconscious Page 2/6

blocks.text: Caring for the Sick Child in the Community (Compressed Test Edition)
A child who is unusually sleepy, difficult to wake, or unconscious has a danger sign. Try to wake the child by clapping or speaking loudly.
3.3 LOOK for signs of severe malnutrition
Use the MUAC (Mid-Upper Arm Circumference) strap on children age 6 months up to 5 years. Wrap the strap around the child's left upper arm, midway between shoulder and elbow. Read the colour:

items: - YELLOW: moderate malnutrition (refer to supplementary feeding if available)
- RED: severe malnutrition - DANGER SIGN - refer urgently

blocks.text: Also check for swelling of both feet (oedema). Press thumbs gently on the top of each foot for 3 seconds. If dents remain on BOTH feet, the child has severe malnutrition. Refer urgently.
4. DECIDE: REFER OR TREAT
4.1 Danger signs requiring URGENT referral
If ANY of these danger signs are present, refer the child urgently:

items: - Not able to drink or feed
- Red on MUAC strap (severe acute malnutrition)

blocks.text: A child with any danger sign is too ill to treat in the community. Give the first dose of medicine before referral if the child can drink. Write a referral note. Arrange transportation.
4.2 No danger sign: TREAT AT HOME
Children without danger signs who have diarrhoea, fever (in a malaria area), or cough with fast breathing may be treated at home.
5. TREATMENT PROTOCOLS
5.1 Diarrhoea (less than 14 days, no blood, no danger sign)
Give ORS (Oral Rehydration Salts) and zinc supplement.
ORS preparation and administration:

items: 1. Wash hands with soap and water. 2. Pour entire ORS packet into a clean container. 3. Add 1 litre of clean water and mix until dissolved. 4. Give frequent small sips from a cup or spoon. 5. If the child vomits, wait 10 minutes, then give more slowly. 6. Continue breastfeeding. Give at least half a cup after each loose stool. 7. Store mixed ORS in a covered container; discard after 24 hours.

blocks.text: Zinc supplement dosage:

blocks.html:
AgeDoseFrequencyDuration / Supply
2 months up to 6 months1/2 tabletOnce daily10 days / 5 tablets
blocks.text: Age Dose Frequency Duration / Supply 2 months up to 6 months 1/2 tablet Once daily 10 days / 5 tablets
Page 3/6

3.raw_text: Caring for the Sick Child in the Community (Compressed Test Edition) A child who is unusually sleepy, difficult to wake, or unconscious has a danger sign. Try to wake the child by clapping or speaking loudly. 3.3 LOOK for signs of severe malnutrition Use the MUAC (Mid-Upper Arm Circumference) strap on children age 6 months up to 5 years. Wrap the strap around the child's left upper arm, midway between shoulder and elbow. Read the colour: - GREEN: not malnourished - YELLOW: moderate malnutrition (refer to supplementary feeding if available) - RED: severe malnutrition - DANGER SIGN - refer urgently Also check for swelling of both feet (oedema). Press thumbs gently on the top of each foot for 3 seconds. If dents remain on BOTH feet, the child has severe malnutrition. Refer urgently. 4. DECIDE: REFER OR TREAT 4.1 Danger signs requiring URGENT referral If ANY of these danger signs are present, refer the child urgently: - Cough for 14 days or more - Diarrhoea for 14 days or more - Blood in stool - Fever for 7 days or more - Convulsions - Not able to drink or feed - Vomits everything - Chest indrawing (severe pneumonia) - Unusually sleepy or unconscious - Red on MUAC strap (severe acute malnutrition) - Swelling of both feet (oedema) A child with any danger sign is too ill to treat in the community. Give the first dose of medicine before referral if the child can drink. Write a referral note. Arrange transportation. 4.2 No danger sign: TREAT AT HOME Children without danger signs who have diarrhoea, fever (in a malaria area), or cough with fast breathing may be treated at home. 5. TREATMENT PROTOCOLS 5.1 Diarrhoea (less than 14 days, no blood, no danger sign) Give ORS (Oral Rehydration Salts) and zinc supplement. ORS preparation and administration: 1. Wash hands with soap and water. 2. Pour entire ORS packet into a clean container. 3. Add 1 litre of clean water and mix until dissolved. 4. Give frequent small sips from a cup or spoon. 5. If the child vomits, wait 10 minutes, then give more slowly. 6. Continue breastfeeding. Give at least half a cup after each loose stool. 7. Store mixed ORS in a covered container; discard after 24 hours. Zinc supplement dosage: Age Dose Frequency Duration / Supply 2 months up to 6 months 1/2 tablet Once daily 10 days / 5 tablets Page 3/6

blocks.text: Caring for the Sick Child in the Community (Compressed Test Edition)

blocks.html:
l
6 months up to 5 years1 tabletOnce daily10 days / 10 tablets
blocks.text: 6 months up to 5 years 1 tablet Once daily 10 days / 10 tablets

Dissolve the zinc tablet (or half tablet) in breast milk or clean water on a spoon. Give the full 10 days even if diarrhoea stops. Zinc reduces severity, shortens duration, and prevents diarrhoea for up to 3 months.
5.2 Fever (less than 7 days) in a malaria area
Do a Rapid Diagnostic Test (RDT) for malaria before treating. Do not give antimalarial medicine unless the RDT is positive.
RDT procedure (summary):

items: 1. Check expiry date; do not use expired tests. 2. Wear new gloves for each child. 3. Clean the child's ring finger with a spirit swab; let dry. 4. Prick the finger with a lancet; collect blood with the loop. 5. Place blood in square hole A on the test strip. 6. Add 6 drops of buffer into round hole B. 7. Wait 15 minutes. 8. Read results: POSITIVE = red lines in both C and T windows. NEGATIVE = red line in C window only. INVALID = no line in C window; repeat with new test.

blocks.text: Antimalarial AL (Artemether-Lumefantrine) dosage:

blocks.html:
AgeDoseFrequencyDuration / Supply
2 months up to 3 years1 tablet ALTwice daily3 days / 6 tablets
3 years up to 5 years2 tablets ALTwice daily3 days /12 tablets
blocks.text: Age Dose Frequency Duration / Supply 2 months up to 3 years 1 tablet AL Twice daily 3 days / 6 tablets 3 years up to 5 years 2 tablets AL Twice daily 3 days / 12 tablets

Give first dose immediately. Second dose 8 hours later. Then morning and evening for 2 more days. Crush tablet and mix with breast milk, water, or food. If the child spits out the entire dose, give another. If unable to take medicine, refer to health facility.
If RDT is negative: do not give antimalarial. Follow up in 3 days. If fever persists at follow-up, refer to health facility.
5.3 Cough with fast breathing (pneumonia, no chest indrawing)
Oral amoxicillin dosage (250 mg tablets):

blocks.html:
AgeDoseFrequencyDuration / Supply
2 months up to 12 months1 tabletTwice daily5 days / 10 tablets
12 months up to 5 years2 tabletsTwice daily5 days / 20 tablets
blocks.text: Age Dose Frequency Duration / Supply 2 months up to 12 months 1 tablet Twice daily 5 days / 10 tablets 12 months up to 5 years 2 tablets Twice daily 5 days / 20 tablets
Check expiration date. Crush the tablet and mix with breast milk or water. Give the first dose in front of you. Give the full 5 days even if the child feels better.
6. HOME CARE ADVICE (for all children treated at home)

items: - Give more fluids and continue feeding.
- Return immediately if the child: cannot drink or feed, becomes sicker, or has blood in stool.
- Keep all medicine out of reach of children.
- Store medicine in a clean, dry place.

blocks.text: 7. FOLLOW-UP VISITS
Follow up ALL children treated at home in 3 days. If the child is not better or is worse at follow-up, refer to a health facility.
7.1 Follow-up for diarrhoea
After 3 days: Is the child still having diarrhoea? If yes and for less than 14 days, continue ORS and zinc. If 14 days or more, refer. Ensure the caregiver is giving zinc for the full 10 days.
7.2 Follow-up for fever / malaria
Page 4/6

4.raw_text: Caring for the Sick Child in the Community (Compressed Test Edition) 6 months up to 5 years 1 tablet Once daily 10 days / 10 tablets Dissolve the zinc tablet (or half tablet) in breast milk or clean water on a spoon. Give the full 10 days even if diarrhoea stops. Zinc reduces severity, shortens duration, and prevents diarrhoea for up to 3 months. 5.2 Fever (less than 7 days) in a malaria area Do a Rapid Diagnostic Test (RDT) for malaria before treating. Do not give antimalarial medicine unless the RDT is positive. RDT procedure (summary): 1. Check expiry date; do not use expired tests. 2. Wear new gloves for each child. 3. Clean the child's ring finger with a spirit swab; let dry. 4. Prick the finger with a lancet; collect blood with the loop. 5. Place blood in square hole A on the test strip. 6. Add 6 drops of buffer into round hole B. 7. Wait 15 minutes. 8. Read results: POSITIVE = red lines in both C and T windows. NEGATIVE = red line in C window only. INVALID = no line in C window; repeat with new test. Antimalarial AL (Artemether-Lumefantrine) dosage: Age Dose Frequency Duration / Supply 2 months up to 3 years 1 tablet AL Twice daily 3 days / 6 tablets 3 years up to 5 years 2 tablets AL Twice daily 3 days / 12 tablets Give first dose immediately. Second dose 8 hours later. Then morning and evening for 2 more days. Crush tablet and mix with breast milk, water, or food. If the child spits out the entire dose, give another. If unable to take medicine, refer to health facility. If RDT is negative: do not give antimalarial. Follow up in 3 days. If fever persists at follow-up, refer to health facility. 5.3 Cough with fast breathing (pneumonia, no chest indrawing) Oral amoxicillin dosage (250 mg tablets): Age Dose Frequency Duration / Supply 2 months up to 12 months 1 tablet Twice daily 5 days / 10 tablets 12 months up to 5 years 2 tablets Twice daily 5 days / 20 tablets Check expiration date. Crush the tablet and mix with breast milk or water. Give the first dose in front of you. Give the full 5 days even if the child feels better. 6. HOME CARE ADVICE (for all children treated at home) - Give more fluids and continue feeding. - Return immediately if the child: cannot drink or feed, becomes sicker, or has blood in stool. - Sleep under an insecticide-treated bednet (ITN). - Keep all medicine out of reach of children. - Store medicine in a clean, dry place. 7. FOLLOW-UP VISITS Follow up ALL children treated at home in 3 days. If the child is not better or is worse at follow-up, refer to a health facility. 7.1 Follow-up for diarrhoea After 3 days: Is the child still having diarrhoea? If yes and for less than 14 days, continue ORS and zinc. If 14 days or more, refer. Ensure the caregiver is giving zinc for the full 10 days. 7.2 Follow-up for fever / malaria Page 4/6

blocks.text: Caring for the Sick Child in the Community (Compressed Test Edition)
After 3 days: Does the child still have fever? If RDT was positive and antimalarial was given, the child should improve. If still febrile, refer. If RDT was negative and fever persists, refer.
7.3 Follow-up for cough with fast breathing
After 3 days: Is the child still coughing? Count breaths again. If still fast breathing, refer. If breathing is normal and child is improving, complete the full 5-day course of amoxicillin.
8. REFERRAL PROCEDURES

When referring a child: 1. Explain to the caregiver why the child must go to the health facility. 2. Write a referral note including: child's name, age, date, reason for referral, danger signs found, treatment already given, and your name. 3. Give the first dose of any appropriate medicine before referral. 4. Help arrange transportation. 5. If fever, keep the child cool during travel. 6. If diarrhoea, give ORS solution during travel. 7. Follow up the child after return, at least once a week until well.
9. CHW MEDICINE KIT AND SUPPLIES
The CHW must maintain the following supplies:
Medicines
Diagnostic equipment

items: - Timer or watch with second hand (for counting breaths)

blocks.text: Other supplies
Page 5/6

5.raw_text: Caring for the Sick Child in the Community (Compressed Test Edition) After 3 days: Does the child still have fever? If RDT was positive and antimalarial was given, the child should improve. If still febrile, refer. If RDT was negative and fever persists, refer. 7.3 Follow-up for cough with fast breathing After 3 days: Is the child still coughing? Count breaths again. If still fast breathing, refer. If breathing is normal and child is improving, complete the full 5-day course of amoxicillin. 8. REFERRAL PROCEDURES When referring a child: 1. Explain to the caregiver why the child must go to the health facility. 2. Write a referral note including: child's name, age, date, reason for referral, danger signs found, treatment already given, and your name. 3. Give the first dose of any appropriate medicine before referral. 4. Help arrange transportation. 5. If fever, keep the child cool during travel. 6. If diarrhoea, give ORS solution during travel. 7. Follow up the child after return, at least once a week until well. 9. CHW MEDICINE KIT AND SUPPLIES The CHW must maintain the following supplies: Medicines - ORS packets - Zinc tablets (20 mg dispersible) - Artemether-Lumefantrine (AL) tablets - Amoxicillin tablets (250 mg) Diagnostic equipment - Rapid Diagnostic Tests (RDT) for malaria - MUAC straps - Timer or watch with second hand (for counting breaths) - Thermometer (optional) Other supplies - Gloves (new pair for each RDT) - Lancets - Spirit swabs (alcohol swabs) - Sick Child Recording Forms - Referral note forms - Buffer solution (for RDTs) - Non-sharps waste container Page 5/6

CHWs identify signs of illness, treat diarrhoea, confirmed malaria, and pneumonia (cough with fast breathing) at home, and urgently refer children with danger signs to a health facility.

not_able_to_drink_or_feed.title: Not able to drink or feed

blocks.text: Not able to drink or feed
A child who cannot drink or feed anything at all has a danger sign. Ask the caregiver to offer the child a drink or breastfeed.

not_able_to_drink_or_feed.raw_text:
Not able to drink or feed
A child who cannot drink or feed anything at all has a danger sign. Ask the caregiver to offer the child a drink or breastfeed.

unusually_sleepy_or_unconscious.title: Unusually sleepy or unconscious

blocks.text: Unusually sleepy or unconscious
Page 2/6
Caring for the Sick Child in the Community (Compressed Test Edition)
A child who is unusually sleepy, difficult to wake, or unconscious has a danger sign. Try to wake the child by clapping or speaking loudly.

unusually_sleepy_or_unconscious.raw_text:
Unusually sleepy or unconscious
Page 2/6
Caring for the Sick Child in the Community (Compressed Test Edition)
A child who is unusually sleepy, difficult to wake, or unconscious has a danger sign. Try to wake the child by clapping or speaking loudly.

ors_preparation_and_administration.title: ORS preparation and administration:

blocks.text: ORS preparation and administration:

items: 1. Wash hands with soap and water. 2. Pour entire ORS packet into a clean container. 3. Add 1 litre of clean water and mix until dissolved. 4. Give frequent small sips from a cup or spoon. 5. If the child vomits, wait 10 minutes, then give more slowly. 6. Continue breastfeeding. Give at least half a cup after each loose stool. 7. Store mixed ORS in a covered container; discard after 24 hours.

ors_preparation_and_administration.raw_text:
ORS preparation and administration:
1. Wash hands with soap and water. 2. Pour entire ORS packet into a clean container. 3. Add 1 litre of clean water and mix until dissolved. 4. Give frequent small sips from a cup or spoon. 5. If the child vomits, wait 10 minutes, then give more slowly. 6. Continue breastfeeding. Give at least half a cup after each loose stool. 7. Store mixed ORS in a covered container; discard after 24 hours.

oral_amoxicillin_dosage_250_mg_tablets.title: Oral amoxicillin dosage (250 mg tablets):

blocks.text: Oral amoxicillin dosage (250 mg tablets):

blocks.html:
AgeDoseFrequencyDuration / Supply
2 months up to 12 months1 tabletTwice daily5 days / 10 tablets
12 months up to 5 years2 tabletsTwice daily5 days / 20 tablets
blocks.text: Age Dose Frequency Duration / Supply 2 months up to 12 months 1 tablet Twice daily 5 days / 10 tablets 12 months up to 5 years 2 tablets Twice daily 5 days / 20 tablets
Check expiration date. Crush the tablet and mix with breast milk or water. Give the first dose in front of you. Give the full 5 days even if the child feels better.
6. HOME CARE ADVICE (for all children treated at home)

items: - Give more fluids and continue feeding.
- Return immediately if the child: cannot drink or feed, becomes sicker, or has blood in stool.
- Keep all medicine out of reach of children.
- Store medicine in a clean, dry place.

blocks.text: 7. FOLLOW-UP VISITS
Follow up ALL children treated at home in 3 days. If the child is not better or is worse at follow-up, refer to a health facility.
7.1 Follow-up for diarrhoea
After 3 days: Is the child still having diarrhoea? If yes and for less than 14 days, continue ORS and zinc. If 14 days or more, refer. Ensure the caregiver is giving zinc for the full 10 days.
7.2 Follow-up for fever / malaria
Page 4/6
Caring for the Sick Child in the Community (Compressed Test Edition)
After 3 days: Does the child still have fever? If RDT was positive and antimalarial was given, the child should improve. If still febrile, refer. If RDT was negative and fever persists, refer.

oral_amoxicillin_dosage_250_mg_tablets.raw_text:
Oral amoxicillin dosage (250 mg tablets):
Age Dose Frequency Duration / Supply 2 months up to 12 months 1 tablet Twice daily 5 days / 10 tablets 12 months up to 5 years 2 tablets Twice daily 5 days / 20 tablets
Check expiration date. Crush the tablet and mix with breast milk or water. Give the first dose in front of you. Give the full 5 days even if the child feels better.
6. HOME CARE ADVICE (for all children treated at home)
- Give more fluids and continue feeding.
- Return immediately if the child: cannot drink or feed, becomes sicker, or has blood in stool.
- Sleep under an insecticide-treated bednet (ITN).
- Keep all medicine out of reach of children.
- Store medicine in a clean, dry place.
7. FOLLOW-UP VISITS
Follow up ALL children treated at home in 3 days. If the child is not better or is worse at follow-up, refer to a health facility.
7.1 Follow-up for diarrhoea
After 3 days: Is the child still having diarrhoea? If yes and for less than 14 days, continue ORS and zinc. If 14 days or more, refer. Ensure the caregiver is giving zinc for the full 10 days.
7.2 Follow-up for fever / malaria
Page 4/6
Caring for the Sick Child in the Community (Compressed Test Edition)
After 3 days: Does the child still have fever? If RDT was positive and antimalarial was given, the child should improve. If still febrile, refer. If RDT was negative and fever persists, refer.

3_3_look_for_signs_of_severe_malnutritio.title: 3.3 LOOK for signs of severe malnutrition

blocks.text: 3.3 LOOK for signs of severe malnutrition
Use the MUAC (Mid-Upper Arm Circumference) strap on children age 6 months up to 5 years. Wrap the strap around the child's left upper arm, midway between shoulder and elbow. Read the colour:

items: - YELLOW: moderate malnutrition (refer to supplementary feeding if available)
- RED: severe malnutrition - DANGER SIGN - refer urgently

blocks.text: Also check for swelling of both feet (oedema). Press thumbs gently on the top of each foot for 3 seconds. If dents remain on BOTH feet, the child has severe malnutrition. Refer urgently.
4. DECIDE: REFER OR TREAT
4.1 Danger signs requiring URGENT referral
If ANY of these danger signs are present, refer the child urgently:

items: - Not able to drink or feed
- Red on MUAC strap (severe acute malnutrition)

blocks.text: A child with any danger sign is too ill to treat in the community. Give the first dose of medicine before referral if the child can drink. Write a referral note. Arrange transportation.
4.2 No danger sign: TREAT AT HOME
Children without danger signs who have diarrhoea, fever (in a malaria area), or cough with fast breathing may be treated at home.
5. TREATMENT PROTOCOLS
5.1 Diarrhoea (less than 14 days, no blood, no danger sign)
Give ORS (Oral Rehydration Salts) and zinc supplement.

3_3_look_for_signs_of_severe_malnutritio.raw_text:
3.3 LOOK for signs of severe malnutrition
Use the MUAC (Mid-Upper Arm Circumference) strap on children age 6 months up to 5 years. Wrap the strap around the child's left upper arm, midway between shoulder and elbow. Read the colour:
- GREEN: not malnourished
- YELLOW: moderate malnutrition (refer to supplementary feeding if available)
- RED: severe malnutrition - DANGER SIGN - refer urgently
Also check for swelling of both feet (oedema). Press thumbs gently on the top of each foot for 3 seconds. If dents remain on BOTH feet, the child has severe malnutrition. Refer urgently.
4. DECIDE: REFER OR TREAT
4.1 Danger signs requiring URGENT referral
If ANY of these danger signs are present, refer the child urgently:
- Cough for 14 days or more
- Diarrhoea for 14 days or more
- Blood in stool
- Fever for 7 days or more
- Convulsions
- Not able to drink or feed
- Vomits everything
- Chest indrawing (severe pneumonia)
- Unusually sleepy or unconscious
- Red on MUAC strap (severe acute malnutrition)
- Swelling of both feet (oedema)
A child with any danger sign is too ill to treat in the community. Give the first dose of medicine before referral if the child can drink. Write a referral note. Arrange transportation.
4.2 No danger sign: TREAT AT HOME
Children without danger signs who have diarrhoea, fever (in a malaria area), or cough with fast breathing may be treated at home.
5. TREATMENT PROTOCOLS
5.1 Diarrhoea (less than 14 days, no blood, no danger sign)
Give ORS (Oral Rehydration Salts) and zinc supplement.

7_3_follow_up_for_cough_with_fast_breath.title: 7.3 Follow-up for cough with fast breathing

blocks.text: 7.3 Follow-up for cough with fast breathing
After 3 days: Is the child still coughing? Count breaths again. If still fast breathing, refer. If breathing is normal and child is improving, complete the full 5-day course of amoxicillin.
8. REFERRAL PROCEDURES

When referring a child: 1. Explain to the caregiver why the child must go to the health facility. 2. Write a referral note including: child's name, age, date, reason for referral, danger signs found, treatment already given, and your name. 3. Give the first dose of any appropriate medicine before referral. 4. Help arrange transportation. 5. If fever, keep the child cool during travel. 6. If diarrhoea, give ORS solution during travel. 7. Follow up the child after return, at least once a week until well.
9. CHW MEDICINE KIT AND SUPPLIES
The CHW must maintain the following supplies:

7.3 Follow-up for cough with fast breathing
After 3 days: Is the child still coughing? Count breaths again. If still fast breathing, refer. If breathing is normal and child is improving, complete the full 5-day course of amoxicillin.
8. REFERRAL PROCEDURES
When referring a child: 1. Explain to the caregiver why the child must go to the health facility. 2. Write a referral note including: child's name, age, date, reason for referral, danger signs found, treatment already given, and your name. 3. Give the first dose of any appropriate medicine before referral. 4. Help arrange transportation. 5. If fever, keep the child cool during travel. 6. If diarrhoea, give ORS solution during travel. 7. Follow up the child after return, at least once a week until well.
9. CHW MEDICINE KIT AND SUPPLIES
The CHW must maintain the following supplies:

5_2_fever_less_than_7_days_in_a_malaria_.title: 5.2 Fever (less than 7 days) in a malaria area

blocks.text: 5.2 Fever (less than 7 days) in a malaria area
Do a Rapid Diagnostic Test (RDT) for malaria before treating. Do not give antimalarial medicine unless the RDT is positive.

5_2_fever_less_than_7_days_in_a_malaria_.raw_text:
5.2 Fever (less than 7 days) in a malaria area
Do a Rapid Diagnostic Test (RDT) for malaria before treating. Do not give antimalarial medicine unless the RDT is positive.

antimalarial_al_artemether_lumefantrine_.title: Antimalarial AL (Artemether-Lumefantrine) dosage:

blocks.text: Antimalarial AL (Artemether-Lumefantrine) dosage:

blocks.html:
AgeDoseFrequencyDuration / Supply
2 months up to 3 years1 tablet ALTwice daily3 days / 6 tablets
3 years up to 5 years2 tablets ALTwice daily3 days /12 tablets
blocks.text: Age Dose Frequency Duration / Supply 2 months up to 3 years 1 tablet AL Twice daily 3 days / 6 tablets 3 years up to 5 years 2 tablets AL Twice daily 3 days / 12 tablets

Give first dose immediately. Second dose 8 hours later. Then morning and evening for 2 more days. Crush tablet and mix with breast milk, water, or food. If the child spits out the entire dose, give another. If unable to take medicine, refer to health facility.
If RDT is negative: do not give antimalarial. Follow up in 3 days. If fever persists at follow-up, refer to health facility.
5.3 Cough with fast breathing (pneumonia, no chest indrawing)

antimalarial_al_artemether_lumefantrine_.raw_text:
Antimalarial AL (Artemether-Lumefantrine) dosage:
Age Dose Frequency Duration / Supply 2 months up to 3 years 1 tablet AL Twice daily 3 days / 6 tablets 3 years up to 5 years 2 tablets AL Twice daily 3 days / 12 tablets
Give first dose immediately. Second dose 8 hours later. Then morning and evening for 2 more days. Crush tablet and mix with breast milk, water, or food. If the child spits out the entire dose, give another. If unable to take medicine, refer to health facility.
If RDT is negative: do not give antimalarial. Follow up in 3 days. If fever persists at follow-up, refer to health facility.
5.3 Cough with fast breathing (pneumonia, no chest indrawing)

based_on_who_unicef_2012_isbn_978_92_4_1.title: Based on: WHO/UNICEF 2012 ISBN 978 92 4 154804 5

blocks.text: Based on: WHO/UNICEF 2012 ISBN 978 92 4 154804 5

This compressed edition preserves all clinical decision logic, danger signs, treatment protocols, dosage tables, and follow-up rules from the original 141-page manual. Exercises, role plays, photos, and facilitation notes are omitted. For training use only with the full manual.
